items.title: dataprof
items.subtitle: High-performance data profiling with ISO 8000/25012 quality metrics.
items.summary: A Rust library, CLI, and Python package for profiling tabular data with bounded memory, format flexibility, and machine-readable quality outputs.

mediaSlots.label: System architecture
mediaSlots.caption: Rust core, interfaces, and report pipeline.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: Profiling should be something teams can run in CI and scripts, not only in notebooks or ad hoc inspection sessions.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: The core combines bounded-memory profiling, column statistics, type and pattern detection, and ISO 8000/25012 quality dimensions across multiple input and export formats.

sections.heading: Current proof points
sections.body: The repo already demonstrates selective metrics for faster runs, bounded-memory streaming, async and database feature flags, and distribution across Crates.io, docs.rs, and PyPI, so the multi-interface story is already visible in public artifacts rather than planned copy.

items.title: Ares + Ceres
items.subtitle: Open-data harvesting and LLM extraction as complementary collection systems.
items.summary: Ceres keeps portal metadata synchronized over time, while Ares turns arbitrary pages into schema-driven structured data with queues, retries, and change detection.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: Harvesting and scraping are different operational contracts, and treating them as the same thing usually makes both systems worse.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: Ceres centers on incremental portal sync and catalog durability, while Ares focuses on fetch pipelines, markdown normalization, JSON Schema extraction, retries, and queue-driven execution.

sections.heading: Current proof points
sections.body: The distinction is already concrete in the READMEs: Ceres offers incremental portal sync, metadata-only mode, export, and optional semantic search, while Ares offers schema-driven extraction, change detection, queue workers, crawl sessions, and protected API endpoints for scrape orchestration.

items.title: Mosaico
items.subtitle: Contribution work on an open data platform for robotics and physical AI.
items.summary: A contribution-oriented case study around workflow boundaries, developer ergonomics, and data-platform integration surfaces in Mosaico.

mediaSlots.label: Platform cover
mediaSlots.caption: Monorepo surface for SDK plus backend.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.body: Robotics and physical-AI systems need data platform primitives that remain understandable once experiments become real operations.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: The interesting work sits at integration boundaries: how orchestration stays flexible enough for experimentation while remaining debuggable for the next engineer.

sections.heading: Current proof points
sections.body: This is already more than a platform summary: the visible contribution history sits exactly at the workflow seams that matter in robotics systems, where CI reliability, query ergonomics, and SDK-server compatibility determine whether data tooling remains usable by the next engineer.

items.title: Zero Grappler
items.subtitle: Minimal no_std async sensor-to-inference pipelines for embedded Rust.
items.summary: A prototype crate for wiring async sources, DSP transforms, and inference stages together through Embassy channels with zero allocations.

mediaSlots.label: Hardware bill
mediaSlots.alt: Zero Grappler hardware cost estimation graphic
mediaSlots.caption: Pico 2 W starter kit and budget.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: Embedded ML examples often entangle hardware access, feature extraction, and inference logic, which makes reuse harder than it should be.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: The crate splits the pipeline into three traits, keeps buffer sizes at compile time, routes stages through Embassy channels, and avoids heap allocation entirely.

sections.heading: Current proof points
sections.body: Even before the first full board demo, the design is already grounded by the no-allocation trait split, monomorphic Embassy task wrappers, the host-side mock example, and a documented bill of materials that keeps the first public hardware loop realistic rather than hypothetical.

items.title: Nephtys
items.subtitle: A real-time connector for live event streams and edge integrations.
items.summary: A Go service that ingests WebSocket, webhook, SSE, and gRPC streams, normalizes events, and publishes them to NATS JetStream with durable persistence and operational metrics.
items.coverText: WebSocket, webhook, SSE, and gRPC ingestion normalized into one durable event pipeline.

mediaSlots.label: REST poller run
mediaSlots.caption: Open-Meteo poller to a JetStream subject.

mediaSlots.label: Connector matrix
mediaSlots.caption: WebSocket, SSE, gRPC, webhook, and REST.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: Live interfaces such as webhooks, WebSockets, SSE, and gRPC all have different operational quirks, but downstream systems still need one stable event contract.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: Nephtys manages connector lifecycles, normalizes inbound events, persists them to NATS JetStream, and exposes a REST API so stream management is operable instead of hardcoded.

sections.heading: Current proof points
sections.body: The operational story is already present in public material: supported connector matrices, middleware ordering, REST management APIs, Prometheus and Grafana support, and a UIC 2026 paper framing urban sensor ingestion as the first concrete evaluation path.

items.title: DCE
items.subtitle: Proactive data contracts enforcement built from dataprof's profiling core.
items.summary: A Rust-native engine for defining, validating, and enforcing data contracts across modern table formats, shifting quality checks from after-the-fact profiling to contract execution.
items.coverText: Move from reactive profiling to explicit contracts that can fail fast inside data workflows.

mediaSlots.label: Contract example
mediaSlots.caption: Iceberg contract fields and quality rules.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: Profiling is useful after the fact, but data platforms also need an explicit way to define what must be true before pipelines keep moving.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: DCE grows out of dataprof by moving from observation to enforcement: contract definitions, validation commands, and table-format-aware checks become the center of the workflow.

sections.heading: Current proof points
sections.body: The shift from profiler to contract engine is already visible in the public repo through Iceberg-aware validation, explicit known limitations for non-Iceberg formats, 194 tests across packages, and a roadmap that makes the next steps in SQL execution and multi-format validation explicit.

items.title: Open Lakehouse Starter
items.subtitle: A small-team lakehouse stack with dlt, dbt, Superset, and MinIO.
items.summary: An open starter environment for small teams that combines ingestion, transformation, storage, and dashboards in a light but expandable lakehouse setup.
items.coverText: A practical starter stack for ingestion, transformation, dashboards, and S3-compatible storage without enterprise overhead.

mediaSlots.label: Bootstrap flow
mediaSlots.alt: Open Lakehouse Starter setup flow card
mediaSlots.caption: venv, compose, dlt, dbt, dashboards.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: Small teams often need a usable lakehouse before they need platform complexity, so the real challenge is choosing a stack that starts light without painting the team into a corner.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: The project combines dlt ingestion, dbt transformations, Superset dashboards, and MinIO-backed object storage into a setup that is intentionally simple to start and expandable later.

sections.heading: Current proof points
sections.body: The repo already contains the first real artifact set: a published screenshot, a runnable compose stack, an example dlt API pipeline, staged dbt models, and local credentials for MinIO and Superset that make the small-team bootstrap path concrete rather than aspirational.

items.title: Peek-a-Boo
items.subtitle: A minimalist AI agent for surgical file search and extraction.
items.summary: A Python agent that uses focused grep and ls operations to answer codebase questions with lower token cost than broad repository scans.
items.coverText: Use surgical file operations instead of broad context dumps when the task is to find one answer fast.

mediaSlots.label: Mission run
mediaSlots.caption: Credential-hunt mission in the test repo.

mediaSlots.label: Tool surface
mediaSlots.caption: Five tools with strict search limits.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: A lot of code search tasks do not need a full-repo embedding or a giant prompt, they need a cheap agent that can ask narrower questions in sequence.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: Peek-a-Boo keeps the tool surface intentionally small, leaning on grep-like operations and focused traversal so the model spends tokens on synthesis instead of raw repository context.

sections.heading: Current proof points
sections.body: The project already behaves like a benchmark-shaped prototype: four built-in missions, a generated target codebase, safety limits that prevent token blowups, and a deliberately narrow tool surface that makes the token-saving argument inspectable in code rather than only in prose.

items.title: druid-datafusion-bridge
items.subtitle: Query Apache Druid segments directly through DataFusion.
items.summary: A Rust bridge that reads Druid segment files directly and exposes them to DataFusion as queryable tables without requiring a running Druid cluster.
items.coverText: Read Druid segment internals directly and surface them as DataFusion tables without standing up the original cluster.

mediaSlots.label: Segment anatomy
mediaSlots.caption: Smoosh files, dictionaries, metrics, and bitmaps.

mediaSlots.label: Query demo
mediaSlots.caption: Register a segment and query it in DataFusion.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: Query engines become more reusable when storage formats can be inspected directly instead of only through the original serving system.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: The library reads Druid segment structures such as index.dr data and maps them into DataFusion table and execution abstractions, aiming for vectorized Arrow-friendly execution where possible.

sections.heading: Current proof points
sections.body: Even as a work in progress, the evaluation surface is already concrete: a dedicated segment parser layer, DataFusion `TableProvider` and `ExecutionPlan` adapters, Wikipedia segment fixtures, and integration tests around offline segment access rather than hand-wavy architectural claims.

items.title: AndreaBozzo
items.subtitle: A public front door that combines portfolio, blog, workbench, generators, and a companion API.
items.summary: This repository is both the personal website and the system behind it: a handcrafted landing page, a Hugo blog, a Rust/WASM knowledge workbench, Go generators for static artifacts, and a small Vercel-hosted GitHub stats API.
items.coverAlt: Architecture diagram of the AndreaBozzo site showing the landing page, Hugo blog, Rust and WebAssembly workbench, Go harvester, GitHub Pages, and Vercel companion API
items.coverText: A single repo that serves as landing page, blog, generated work archive, and companion API surface instead of a static brochure site.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: A personal site should not be a thin profile page if the work itself is systems work. This repository is meant to function as a public front door, a writing archive, and a concrete example of how I like to structure tools and platforms.

sections.heading: System shape
sections.body: The root site is plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, the long-form writing lives in a Hugo blog, the interactive workbench logic is mirrored between JavaScript and Rust compiled to WebAssembly, and generated work pages come from structured JSON rather than hand-maintained HTML.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: The repository now uses a Go harvester to generate case-study and contribution artifacts, GitHub Actions to assemble and publish the static site on GitHub Pages, and a small Go API on Vercel to expose live GitHub stats and badge endpoints that the homepage can consume.

sections.heading: Current proof points
sections.body: The repository already exposes the whole public system path: structured JSON and the Go harvester generate work and contribution artifacts, Hugo builds the blog, the workbench logic is mirrored in JavaScript and Rust/WASM, and GitHub Pages plus the Vercel API split static delivery from live GitHub stats.
